personal_information.how_often_do_you_check_email: I check my personal email 2–3 times a day (morning before work, sometimes at lunch, and usually once again in the evening). I check my work email continuously throughout the workday via Outlook on my computer and phone.
personal_information.would_you_like_your_weight_to_be_differe: Yes. I would like to reduce my weight to around 185–190 lbs over the next 12–18 months in a sustainable way. I carry most of my weight around my midsection, and I’ve noticed that as I’ve gotten into my late 40s and now 50, losing weight has become harder even though I don’t feel like I’m eating dramatically more. My main goals are: (1) lose about 20–25 lbs of fat while maintaining or even increasing muscle mass, (2) improve my waist circumference and get back into size 34 pants (I’m currently a snug 36), and (3) stabilize my weight rather than yo-yoing up and down. I’d like to do this without extreme dieting—ideally by dialing in my nutrition, cutting back on late-night snacking and alcohol, and being more consistent with strength training and cardio. I’m also interested in learning what’s realistic for my age and lifestyle so I don’t chase an unrealistic number but instead focus on feeling strong and energetic.

social_information.children: Two children: son age 14 (Ethan), daughter age 11 (Sophie). Both live at home full-time.
social_information.pets: One 6-year-old black Labrador retriever ("Milo") and a 3-year-old tabby cat ("Luna").
social_information.occupation: Senior project manager in a mid-sized software company (primarily desk/computer-based work, some travel 3–4 times per year).

health_information.please_list_your_main_health_concerns:
1. Low energy and fatigue, especially in the late afternoon and early evening. I often hit a wall around 3–4 pm and rely on coffee or something sweet to push through the rest of the workday. By the time I’m home, I feel mentally fried and don’t have much motivation to exercise or be active.

2. Increasing belly fat and gradual weight gain over the last few years. I’ve gone from about 190 lbs in my late 30s to just over 210 lbs now. Most of the extra weight is around my midsection. I’m concerned about long-term health risks like heart disease and diabetes, especially with a family history of high blood pressure and cholesterol.

3. Sleep quality. I usually fall asleep quickly but wake up 2–3 times per night, sometimes to use the bathroom and sometimes for no obvious reason. My wife says I snore moderately, and on a few occasions she’s noticed I seem to stop breathing for a few seconds and then snort awake. I often wake up feeling not fully rested, even after 7–8 hours in bed.

4. Joint stiffness and occasional pain, particularly in my knees and lower back. My knees can feel tight and a bit sore after playing basketball with my son or going on longer hikes. My lower back gets tight when I sit for long periods at my desk. I haven’t had serious injuries, but I’m noticing more aches and pains than I did even 5–10 years ago.

5. Libido and sexual health. Over the last 2–3 years I’ve noticed a gradual decrease in libido and sometimes difficulty maintaining erections, especially when I’m stressed or very tired. It’s not constant, but it’s enough that it’s on my mind. I haven’t talked to a doctor about it yet because it’s a bit embarrassing, but I suspect it might be related to stress, sleep, and possibly hormones.

6. Stress and work-life balance. My job can be demanding, with multiple projects and deadlines, and I tend to internalize stress rather than talk about it. I don’t have full-blown anxiety or depression, but I can feel ‘on edge’ or mentally overloaded. I notice that when I’m stressed, I snack more, drink more beer or wine in the evenings, and am less likely to exercise.

Overall, I want to address these issues before they turn into something more serious. I’d like to feel more energetic, lose the extra weight, sleep better, manage stress in healthier ways, and support my long-term health as I move into my 50s.
health_information.other_concerns_and_or_goals:
In addition to my main concerns, I have a few broader goals:

1. Build a sustainable exercise routine. Right now my activity level is inconsistent. Some weeks I’ll get in 2–3 walks or a bike ride, and other weeks I’ll do almost nothing. I’d like a realistic plan that fits my schedule—ideally 3 days of strength training and 2 days of moderate cardio (like brisk walking, cycling, or hiking) that I can stick with long term. I’m not trying to become a bodybuilder, but I’d like to feel stronger and more physically capable.

2. Improve nutrition and relationship with food. I tend to eat fairly well at breakfast and lunch, but my weak spots are portion control at dinner, second helpings, and late-night snacking (chips, cheese, crackers, or sweets) while watching TV. I also drink beer or wine 3–4 nights a week (usually 1–2 drinks). I’d like guidance on meal planning, realistic portion sizes for my age and activity level, and strategies for managing cravings in the evenings without feeling deprived.

3. Better stress management. I don’t currently have a structured way to manage stress. I sometimes go for a walk or listen to music, but it’s not consistent. I’m interested in learning simple practices like breathing exercises, short meditations, or routines I can do during the workday to reset my mind so I don’t carry as much tension into the evening.

4. Long-term health and prevention. My dad developed high blood pressure and elevated cholesterol in his 50s, and my maternal grandfather had a heart attack in his early 60s. I want to be proactive about my heart health, blood sugar, and hormone levels. I’d like to understand what lab tests I should be paying attention to and how lifestyle changes (food, movement, sleep, stress) can reduce my risk.

5. Being a better role model for my kids. My son is very into sports, and my daughter is starting to get interested in fitness and body image. I’d like to model a healthy, balanced approach to food, exercise, and self-care, rather than the ‘all or nothing’ mindset I sometimes fall into. I want them to see me prioritizing my health without obsessing over it.

6. Structure and accountability. I tend to start health kicks and then lose momentum after a few weeks when work gets busy or life gets stressful. I’m looking for more structure, realistic goal-setting, and some accountability so that these changes become part of my lifestyle instead of short-term bursts.

Overall, my goal is not just to fix one problem but to build a healthier routine that supports my energy, mood, and long-term health, and that fits with being a busy working dad and husband.
health_information.at_what_point_in_your_life_did_you_feel_:
Physically, I felt my best in my early to mid-30s, roughly between ages 30 and 36. At that time, I was living in Madison and then Denver, had recently gotten married, and was still playing pickup basketball twice a week and going to the gym fairly regularly. I weighed around 185–190 lbs, my waist was about 33–34 inches, and I remember feeling strong, relatively lean, and confident in my body.

My schedule was a bit more flexible then, and we didn’t have kids yet, so I had more time and mental bandwidth to focus on working out and cooking at home. I usually went to the gym 3 times a week for strength training and did some form of cardio on most non-gym days—either biking to work, going for a run, or playing basketball. I also slept more consistently, averaging 7.5–8 hours a night, and I didn’t wake up as much during the night.

Mentally and emotionally, I felt a strong sense of momentum and optimism. Work was challenging but not overwhelming, and I didn’t have as many competing responsibilities as I do now with kids, a mortgage, and a more senior role at work. My stress levels felt manageable, and I had more energy and motivation to be active in my free time.

I also noticed that my body recovered faster back then. If I played a hard game of basketball or did a tough workout, I might feel a bit sore the next day but I bounced back quickly. Now, in my 50s, if I push too hard without proper warm-up or recovery, I can feel it for several days, especially in my knees and lower back.

Overall, that period in my early to mid-30s felt like the best combination of physical fitness, energy, sleep, and mental well-being. My goal isn’t necessarily to ‘go back’ to that exact version of myself, but to recapture some of that feeling of strength, energy, and confidence in a way that makes sense for my current age and responsibilities.
health_information.any_serious_illnesses_hospitalizations_i:
I’ve been relatively fortunate with my health, but there are a few notable events:

1. Appendectomy (1992): At age 18, I had an emergency appendectomy after sudden severe abdominal pain. I was hospitalized for 3 days for surgery and recovery. There were no major complications, and I haven’t had any long-term issues from it.

2. Right ankle sprain (2008): I severely sprained my right ankle playing pickup basketball. I didn’t break any bones, but I tore some ligaments and was on crutches for about a week. I did about 6 weeks of physical therapy. It still occasionally feels a bit weak or stiff if I do a lot of lateral movement, but it doesn’t limit me much day to day.

3. Lower back strain (2016): I strained my lower back lifting a heavy box while helping a friend move. I didn’t go to the hospital, but I did see my primary care doctor and then a physical therapist for a few sessions. It took about 4–6 weeks to feel mostly normal again. Since then, I’m prone to mild lower back tightness if I sit too long or lift improperly, which is part of why I want to strengthen my core and improve my posture.

4. COVID-19 infection (2021): I tested positive for COVID-19 in January 2021. Symptoms were moderate—fever, fatigue, body aches, and loss of taste and smell for about 10 days. I was treated at home and did not require hospitalization. I recovered fully, but I did notice that my stamina was reduced for a couple of months afterward. I don’t have any known long-term complications now.

5. Elevated blood pressure (recent monitoring): I haven’t been hospitalized for this, but at my last two annual physicals (2023 and 2024), my blood pressure readings have been in the borderline high range (around 132–138/84–88). My doctor hasn’t started me on medication yet but has recommended lifestyle changes (weight loss, exercise, reducing sodium, moderating alcohol) and regular monitoring. This is one of the reasons I’m taking my health more seriously now.

Other than these, I’ve had the usual minor illnesses (colds, occasional flu) but nothing requiring hospitalization. No history of heart attacks, strokes, cancer, or major surgeries besides the appendectomy.
health_information.how_is_was_the_health_of_your_mother:
My mother, Susan, is 74 years old and currently living in Madison, WI. Overall, her health is fair but somewhat complicated by a few chronic conditions.

She has a long history of high blood pressure (diagnosed in her late 40s) and has been on medication for it for many years. Her blood pressure is reasonably well controlled when she takes her meds consistently, but there have been periods where she’s been less consistent and her numbers creep up. She also has high cholesterol and takes a statin.

About five years ago, she was diagnosed with type 2 diabetes. It’s been managed primarily with oral medication and some dietary changes, although she still struggles with sweets and portion control. Her A1C has fluctuated but is generally in the mildly elevated range. She’s overweight and carries most of her weight in her midsection, which is something I see echoed in myself.

She also has osteoarthritis in her knees and hands, which limits her mobility somewhat. She walks with a bit of stiffness, especially in the morning or after sitting for a long time. She had one minor fall about two years ago but did not break anything.

Mentally, she’s sharp overall, though she sometimes has minor memory lapses (misplacing items, forgetting small details) that seem typical for her age. No formal diagnosis of dementia or anything like that.

She has never smoked and rarely drinks alcohol. Her main challenges have been weight, blood pressure, cholesterol, and now diabetes. Watching her navigate these issues as she’s gotten older has made me more aware of my own risk factors and motivated me to be more proactive about my health in my 50s.

She is currently independent in her daily activities, still drives, and lives with my stepfather. They walk around their neighborhood a few times a week, but she doesn’t do structured exercise. Her doctor has encouraged more movement and continued monitoring of blood sugar, blood pressure, and cholesterol.
health_information.how_is_was_the_health_of_your_father:
My father, Robert, passed away at age 69 in 2018 from complications related to a heart attack. His health history is a big reason I’m paying closer attention to my own.

He had high blood pressure starting in his early 50s and high cholesterol as well. He was on medication for both but wasn’t always consistent with taking them or following dietary recommendations. He was a former smoker—he smoked about a pack a day from his late teens until his early 40s, then quit for good. He also enjoyed beer and whiskey, sometimes more than was ideal, especially on weekends.

In his early 60s, he was diagnosed with coronary artery disease after experiencing chest discomfort while shoveling snow. He had a stent placed and was advised to exercise more, improve his diet, and lose some weight. He made some changes for a while—more walking, fewer fried foods—but over time he slipped back into old habits, especially around food portions and regular exercise.

He was slightly overweight for most of his adult life (carrying extra weight around his midsection, similar to me), and he had occasional issues with acid reflux. He also had some joint pain in his knees and lower back but nothing that required surgery.

He had a heart attack in late 2017, which he survived but which significantly weakened his heart. After that, his energy level dropped, and he had more difficulty with simple activities like climbing stairs or walking long distances. In mid-2018, he had another cardiac event and passed away in the hospital.

There is no known history of cancer or diabetes in his case, but the cardiovascular issues were significant and, in my opinion, somewhat preventable or at least manageable with more consistent lifestyle changes and medical follow-up.

Knowing this history makes me particularly concerned about my own blood pressure, cholesterol, and weight, especially as I’m now in the same age range where his problems really started to show up. I’d like to take a more proactive and sustainable approach so that I can hopefully avoid following the same path.
